title: Scoping, Estimating & Delivering AI & Data Missions
pitchHtml: You now know the landscape, the methods, the platform, and the law. This module is where you turn all of it into a single client engagement that lands on time, on budget, and provably worked — and where you don't lose your shirt doing it. It is the practitioner-leader's delivery playbook: how to qualify a real opportunity from AI theatre, scope an outcome instead of a technology, estimate work whose hardest parts are genuinely unknowable up front, price and stage it so risk is shared, control the cost of compute and tokens before the bill shocks anyone, and run the delivery in a way that survives the iterative, uncertain nature of ML. The recurring question of this path here becomes brutally concrete: what exactly are you committing to, what does it cost to deliver, and what must you refuse to promise? The leadership and team foundations sit in ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership (AI Scientist Path); this module extends them from "lead a team" into "scope, price, and run a paid mission."

items.html: Qualify an opportunity with a use-case canvas and a value × feasibility score, killing AI theatre early
Scope a mission around an outcome and a "definition of done", staged PoC → pilot → production
Estimate AI/data work under genuine uncertainty — sizing the data work explicitly and communicating ranges, not false precision
Apply a build-vs-buy framework to a specific capability, extending the basics in ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership
Forecast and control the cost of AI (compute, tokens, storage, people, licences) and frame a defensible TCO and ROI
Choose an engagement model (T&M / fixed / phased) and staff the right roles at the right phase
Run delivery with agile adapted to ML's iterative reality, managing stakeholder expectations throughout

subsections.title: Discovery & Qualification: Real Opportunity vs AI Theatre

blocks.html:
Most AI requests arrive as a solution ("we want a chatbot / an agent / a forecasting model"), not a problem. Your first job is to reverse that — find the decision or action that changes, and whether it's worth changing. The questions that separate signal from theatre:

  • What decision or action changes if this works, and how often does it happen? No downstream action ⇒ no value, however clever the model.
  • What is it worth? Put a number on it (€ saved, hours removed, revenue unlocked, risk reduced). "Innovation" is not a number.
  • Who owns the pain and will act on the output? A use case with no owner who feels the problem dies after the demo.
  • Does the data exist, lawfully, and is it usable? Callback to AI & Data Landscape & Tech-Lead Mindset: no usable data → no AI. This is a gate, not a footnote.
  • What do you do today, and what's actually wrong with it? Sometimes the honest answer is "fix the process" or "buy a €30/month SaaS."
  • How will we know it worked? If success can't be measured, it can't be delivered or sold.

The use-case canvas — one page you fill in with the client, before any proposal:

FieldWhat goes hereTheatre smell if…
OutcomeThe decision/action that changes, in business terms"Use AI", "be innovative"
Value€ / hours / risk, with a rough magnitudeNo number, or "priceless"
Trigger & volumeWhen it fires, how many times/monthHappens 5×/month (let a human do it)
User & ownerWho consumes the output and acts on itNo named owner
DataSource, access, lawful basis, rough quality"We'll figure out the data later"
Success metricThe number that proves it, online and offline"It'll feel better"
Failure toleranceCost of a wrong/hallucinated output; human-in-the-loop?Zero-tolerance + full autonomy

Value × feasibility scoring. Score each candidate 1-5 on value (the canvas's € magnitude × confidence) and feasibility (data readiness, technical maturity, governance, change effort), then plot on the 2×2 from AI & Data Landscape & Tech-Lead Mindset:

Low feasibilityHigh feasibility
High valueStrategic bets — stage as discovery + PoC, expect to kill someDo now — your flagship missions
Low valueDrop — politely decline; this is where trust is builtQuick wins — automate cheaply, fund the ambitious ones

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Qualification is expected-value triage, not optimism. The same EV≈P(success)×value−costEV \approx P(\text{success}) \times \text{value} - \text{cost}EV≈P(success)×value−cost lens from module 01 applies per opportunity: a frontier idea with huge value but 20% feasibility and high cost may have lower expected value than a boring automation that's near-certain and cheap. You don't need to compute it precisely — you need to force the three terms onto the table before anyone signs.

subsections.title: Scoping a Mission: Outcome, Stages, Definition of Done

blocks.html:
A scoped mission answers four things before work starts: what outcome, what proves it, what's delivered, and when it's done.

  • Define the outcome and success criteria up front. Write the acceptance test before the build: "The model flags ≥ 80% of fraudulent transactions at ≤ 2% false-positive rate, validated on a held-out month, and the ops team acts on the queue." Both an offline metric and a business condition. Crucially — agree it in writing with the buyer.
  • Surface data prerequisites as gates, not assumptions. "Access to 12 months of labelled transactions by week 1" is a dependency the client owns. Make it explicit; a slipped data gate is the #1 cause of blown timelines (callback: most AI projects are ~70% data work).
  • Stage it: PoC → pilot → production. Each stage has a different question, audience, and exit:

StageQuestion it answersAudienceExit criterionTypical length
DiscoveryIs this worth doing, and how?SponsorScored canvas + plan + estimate1-3 weeks
PoCIs it feasible at all?Tech sponsorMetric hit on sample data, offline2-6 weeks
PilotDoes it work for real users, safely?Users + ownerShadow/limited live, business metric moves1-3 months
ProductionDoes it run reliably & affordably?OpsMonitored, on-budget, handed overOngoing

The staging is your risk management: each gate is a real go/no-go where the client can stop having spent little. Never let a PoC's throwaway code silently become production — see the trap in §7.

  • A crisp "definition of done." For each deliverable, state what "done" means in observable terms: code merged + tests passing, model registered with a model card, monitoring live, runbook written, owner trained. "The model works" is not a definition of done.
  • Avoiding scope creep. Write down what's out of scope as explicitly as what's in. New ideas mid-mission are routed to a backlog and a change request, not absorbed silently. A one-line rule that saves margins: "In scope is what's in the signed canvas; everything else is a change request."

blocks.title: scoping a support-deflection assistant
blocks.html:
A client wants "an AI that answers customer questions." You run the canvas with them and reframe:

  • Outcome: deflect tier-1 support tickets so agents handle fewer repetitive questions. Value: ~40% of 10k monthly tickets are repetitive; at ~€6 fully-loaded cost/ticket, deflecting half is ~€120k/year.
  • Data gate: 18 months of resolved tickets + the help-centre articles, accessible by week 2 (client-owned dependency).
  • Staging: Discovery (2 wks) scores feasibility and picks RAG over fine-tuning (see RAG & Knowledge Systems). PoC (4 wks): on 500 historical questions, the assistant must give a correct, grounded answer ≥ 70% of the time, offline-judged. Pilot (8 wks): deflect on 10% of live traffic with a "talk to a human" escape and answer-quality monitoring. Production: rollout + monitoring + handover.
  • Definition of done (pilot): measured deflection rate, hallucination rate under threshold, escalation path working, owner trained on the content-update loop.
  • What you do not promise: a specific deflection % before the PoC, or zero hallucinations ever. You promise a method for finding the number and a guardrail for the failure mode.

AI/data estimation is hard for reasons that don't apply to ordinary software: you're estimating research (will this approach even hit the metric?) and data work whose size you can't see until you open the data (quality, gaps, labelling effort). Treating these like a known CRUD app is how fixed-price ML missions lose money.

  • Estimate the data work explicitly, as its own line. Acquisition, access, cleaning, labelling, and feature work routinely dominate. If you remember one number, remember that the modelling is often the small part. Give data its own T-shirt size and its own dependency gates.
  • T-shirt sizing over fake precision. Size each workstream S/M/L/XL with an explicit basis ("M = ~3-5 person-weeks, assumes data is reasonably clean"). Resist the false confidence of "37.5 days."
  • Discovery-phase-first is the antidote. You cannot responsibly fixed-price the unknown. Sell a short paid discovery (see Mission Patterns) that converts unknowns into knowns — then estimate the build with real information. This is the single most effective de-risking move in this module.
  • Communicate ranges, not points. Present estimates as ranges tied to assumptions and a confidence level: "6-10 weeks if the data is as described; we'll re-baseline after the 2-week discovery." A point estimate you don't believe is a liability you'll be held to.

WorkstreamWhy it's uncertainHow to size it honestly
Data acquisition & accessPermissions, systems, lawful basis unknown until you askGate + range; client owns the dependency
Data quality & cleaningInvisible until you open the dataDiscovery spike first; size after
LabellingVolume × cost-per-label × QA loopsEstimate per-label cost; pilot on a subset
Modelling / research"Will it hit the metric?" is genuinely openTime-boxed PoC with a kill criterion
Integration & MLOpsOften underestimated; see MLOps & ProductionizationTreat as real software; don't tack on at the end

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Your estimate's error is dominated by the least-known workstream, not the average one. Adding a precise figure for the easy modelling part next to a hand-wave for data quality doesn't make the total precise — the unknown swamps it. The cure isn't more padding; it's converting the biggest unknown into a known via a cheap discovery spike before you commit to a number.

subsections.title: Build vs Buy for This Mission

blocks.html:
ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership gives the general build-vs-buy table and the complexity-budget ROI calculation (improvement × impact vs engineering + infra cost). Don't re-derive it — apply it to the specific capability in front of you, and add the dimension that table omits: cost evolution over time (see §6).

The path's stance — buy the commodity, build the differentiator — operationalised:

CapabilityDefaultWhy
Foundation modelBuy (hosted API)Commodity; you can't out-train a frontier lab
Vector store / embeddingsBuy / OSSCommodity infra; differentiation is your content, not the store
OCR, transcription, translationBuySolved; per-call cost beats build
Your proprietary data & domain workflowBuildThis is the defensible value — nobody else has it
Integration into the client's processBuildGeneric by definition for the client; specific in practice
Trust/eval/guardrail layer for your use caseBuild (thin)Off-the-shelf gets you 80%; the last 20% is yours

The build-vs-buy advisory is itself a sellable mission (see Mission Patterns). The trap to name for clients: "build" feels cheaper because the recurring API line is visible while engineers' fully-loaded cost and maintenance are not.

When you buy, choose deliberately. The failure mode is picking on a demo and discovering the lock-in later.

  • Evaluation criteria: fit-for-task quality, latency, price at your expected volume (not list price), data terms (residency, training-on-your-data, retention), reliability/SLA, security/compliance posture, and exit cost.
  • Run a bake-off / POC on your data. Vendor benchmarks are marketing. Define a small fixed eval set from the real use case (callback to Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation), run 2-3 candidates head-to-head, score on quality and cost-per-unit. A weekend bake-off beats a quarter of regret.
  • Avoid lock-in deliberately: prefer an abstraction layer over provider SDKs so swapping a model is a config change; keep prompts/data/evals portable; watch egress fees and proprietary formats; favour open standards where the cost is small. Lock-in isn't always wrong — but enter it knowingly, with a switching cost you've estimated.

This is the part inexperienced teams and clients systematically forget — and the one most likely to turn a "successful" model into a money pit. FinOps for AI is making cost a first-class, monitored engineering signal, not a quarterly surprise.

The cost drivers:

DriverWhat runs it upLever to control it
Compute / GPUTraining & inference on accelerators; idle reserved capacityRight-size; spot/auto-scale; distil/quantise (see ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership); batch when latency allows
LLM API tokensPer-token in+out × volume; long contexts; chatty agentsSmaller models for easy calls (routing); prompt caching; cap context; cache answers; trim retrieved chunks
Storage & dataWarehouse, vectors, feature store, logs, egressLifecycle/TTL policies; tiering; watch egress between clouds
PeopleFully-loaded engineer/DS time — usually the biggest lineDon't build the commodity; reuse; staff to phase (§8)
Licences / SaaSPer-seat or per-call platform feesMatch plan to real usage; consolidate

TCO — count the whole iceberg. The visible API/compute bill is the tip. Total cost of ownership = build + run (compute, tokens, storage) + maintenance (monitoring, retraining, dependency churn) + licences + the people to operate it. A model is a liability with a monthly invoice for as long as it lives.

A simple ROI / payback framing (use one formula, then talk in plain € — this is the module's first of at most two display expressions):

blocks.tex: \text{Annual ROI} = \frac{(\text{annual benefit}) - (\text{annual run \& maintenance cost})}{\text{build cost}}, \qquad \text{Payback (months)} = \frac{\text{build cost}}{\text{monthly net benefit}}
blocks.html: Annual ROI=(annual benefit)−(annual run & maintenance cost)build cost,Payback (months)=build costmonthly net benefit\text{Annual ROI} = \frac{(\text{annual benefit}) - (\text{annual run \& maintenance cost})}{\text{build cost}}, \qquad \text{Payback (months)} = \frac{\text{build cost}}{\text{monthly net benefit}}Annual ROI=build cost(annual benefit)−(annual run & maintenance cost)​,Payback (months)=monthly net benefitbuild cost​

Worked: the support assistant from §2 — benefit ~€120k/year; build ~€60k; run (tokens + hosting + monitoring) ~€18k/year; maintenance ~€12k/year. Net annual = €120k − €30k = €90k ⇒ ROI ≈ 150%/yr, payback ≈ 8 months. Compare that against the same numbers if tokens were 5× (chatty, uncached): run jumps to ~€90k/year, net collapses to ~€18k, payback stretches past 3 years — the engineering decision and the business case are the same decision.

Cost-per-unit — the number to track and to put in the SLA. Formalise the unit-economics idea above into one figure:

blocks.tex: \text{cost per unit} = \frac{\text{total monthly AI cost}}{\text{units served per month}}
blocks.html: cost per unit=total monthly AI costunits served per month\text{cost per unit} = \frac{\text{total monthly AI cost}}{\text{units served per month}}cost per unit=units served per monthtotal monthly AI cost​

where the unit is the thing the business counts — a request, a resolved ticket, or 1k tokens — and the numerator is all the cost for that period (compute + tokens + storage + the slice of people/licences you allocate to it). Worked: €7,500/month of run cost over 50,000 tickets ⇒ €0.15 per ticket — set that against the ~€6 human cost to see the deflection economics at a glance.

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Totals hide trouble; a unit cost exposes it. A bill that looks fine in aggregate can mean a per-ticket cost quietly climbing as contexts grow or an agent gets chattier — the total only spikes once volume catches up. Tracking cost-per-unit turns spend into a rate you can forecast (multiply by projected volume), benchmark against the human/legacy cost, and commit to: it's the right thing to put in an SLA and on the dashboard next to the quality metric, because the two move together.

Forecasting & controlling spend (the FinOps loop): estimate unit economics up front (cost per request / per ticket / per 1k tokens), then in production tag spend by feature, set budgets and alerts, watch a cost-per-unit dashboard next to the quality dashboard, and review monthly. Make one engineer accountable for the AI bill the way one is accountable for latency.

The "GenAI bill shock" failure mode. A demo costs cents, so nobody models the per-call economics; at production volume — long contexts, multi-step agents, no caching, biggest model for every call — the monthly bill arrives at 10-100× the forecast and the project is "paused." Prevent it by computing cost-per-transaction at expected volume during the PoC, not after launch, and by making token/compute cost an explicit acceptance criterion.

Build-vs-buy economics over time — the crossover volume. Buy is cheaper to start (no build, pay-as-you-go) but the recurring bill scales with usage; build is expensive up front but its marginal cost can flatten. Set total build cost against cumulative buy cost at a volume nnn and solve for where they meet:

build_fixed+n⋅build_marginal=n⋅buy_unit_price⟹n∗=build_fixedbuy_unit_price−build_marginal\text{build\_fixed} + n\cdot\text{build\_marginal} = n\cdot\text{buy\_unit\_price} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad n^{*} = \frac{\text{build\_fixed}}{\text{buy\_unit\_price} - \text{build\_marginal}}build_fixed+n⋅build_marginal=n⋅buy_unit_price⟹n∗=buy_unit_price−build_marginalbuild_fixed​

where build_fixed\text{build\_fixed}build_fixed is the one-off build + setup cost, build_marginal\text{build\_marginal}build_marginal is your per-unit cost to run what you built (compute, your own tokens, the people slice), and buy_unit_price\text{buy\_unit\_price}buy_unit_price is the vendor's per-unit charge. n∗n^{*}n∗ is the crossover volume: below it, buy is cheaper; above it, build is. (If build_marginal≥buy_unit_price\text{build\_marginal} \ge \text{buy\_unit\_price}build_marginal≥buy_unit_price there is no crossover — building never wins, so buy.)

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: The build's fixed cost is a bet you only recoup on volume, and each unit you serve in-house saves the gap between the vendor's price and your own marginal cost — so n∗n^{*}n∗ is just "fixed cost ÷ saving per unit," the point where accumulated savings have repaid the build. The decision must ride on projected volume, not today's: a capability handling 5k calls/month now but 500k next year crosses over even though today's number says buy. Below crossover (most early missions) buy; only persistently far above it, with stable requirements, does build pay — and remember build_marginal\text{build\_marginal}build_marginal must include maintenance, the line teams forget.
Name the risks on the canvas and assign each a mitigation and an owner. The ones specific to AI/data:

RiskWhat it looks likeMitigation
Data riskWrong, late, biased, or unlawful dataData gate in week 1; quality spike in discovery; governance from day one (Data Governance, Privacy & Pseudonymisation)
Model riskDoesn't hit the metric; drifts; degradesTime-boxed PoC with a kill criterion; monitoring + retraining plan (MLOps & Productionization)
The "PoC that never productionises" trapA great demo on a laptop; nobody can deploy/operate it; throwaway code becomes prodGate PoC→production explicitly; budget the productionisation as real work; never quietly ship PoC code
Vendor / model dependencyA single provider's price, terms, or availability can sink youAbstraction layer; a tested fallback model; estimated switching cost (§5)
Change-of-model riskThe provider deprecates or silently updates the model; your prompts/evals regressPin versions where possible; keep a regression eval set; budget for periodic re-validation
Scope / expectation risk"Can it also just…"; success redefined as "use AI"Written definition of done; change-request discipline (§2)
The two you'll under-price by default: productionisation (the demo is 20% of the work) and maintenance (a live model is never "finished").
Which contract shape depends on how much is known — which is exactly what discovery resolves:

ModelUse whenRisk sits withWatch out for
Time & materialsHigh uncertainty (research, messy data)ClientNeeds trust + transparency; cap it
Fixed priceGenuinely well-understood, bounded workYouDeadly for un-scoped ML; only after discovery
Phased / cappedThe norm for AI/dataSharedManage each gate as a real go/no-go

The pragmatic default: fixed-price the discovery, then phase the rest with go/no-go gates — small fixed pieces where each phase is scoped from the previous one's findings. This aligns incentives and shares the risk that no estimate can fully remove.

Staff to the phase, not to the org chart (roles per AI & Data Landscape & Tech-Lead Mindset and ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership):

PhaseWho you actually needWho you don't yet
DiscoveryTech lead + a senior generalist (DS-leaning)A full team
PoCData scientist / AI engineer + data engineer for the pipeMLOps, ops
Pilot+ ML/AI engineer to harden; + the client's product ownerA second model
Production+ MLOps/platform; + ops handover; DS drops to part-timeThe whole PoC team kept idle

blocks.title: Intuition
blocks.html: Staffing is a coverage problem over the current phase, not a fixed headcount (echoing module 01). The classic waste is carrying a four-person discovery team into a phase that needs two, or — the inverse — staffing a production handover with researchers who've never run a service. Match the people to the question the phase is answering.

Managing a small delivery team. Shared ownership of the production metric (not "my model" / "your pipeline"); ML-aware code review (check for data leakage, honest evaluation, reproducibility — Model Evaluation, Validation & Experimentation); and the cultural default this whole path argues for — reward impact, not complexity. The engineer who replaces a model with a SQL rule should be celebrated, not sidelined.

subsections.title: Running Delivery: Agile Adapted for ML

blocks.html:
Standard sprint mechanics work, but ML/data delivery is iterative and uncertain in ways software isn't: a sprint can end with "the approach doesn't hit the metric," which is a valid, valuable result, not a failure. Adapt accordingly:

  • Time-box research as spikes with a question and a kill criterion ("by Friday: can we beat the rule baseline on the sample? if not, we pivot"). This protects the budget from open-ended tinkering.
  • Track learning, not just velocity. Early increments produce de-risked knowledge (data quality understood, baseline established), not always shippable features. Make that progress visible to the sponsor so "no model yet" doesn't read as "no progress."
  • Demo on real (sample) data every cycle, never on a cherry-picked example — demos hide the long tail (callback to module 01's red flag).
  • Manage stakeholder expectations continuously, at three altitudes (executive / manager / engineer — the 3-level explanation from ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership). Re-baseline the range after discovery and again after the PoC; surprises kill trust faster than bad news does. Say "so what does this mean for the business?" every time you show a metric.

These three are the sellable wrappers around this module's craft — and the natural first steps that de-risk everything that follows.

  • Paid Discovery & Scoping Sprint (the sellable first step) — Problem: the client wants a build but nobody can responsibly estimate it. Deliverable: a completed use-case canvas, value × feasibility score, data-gate assessment, a recommended PoC → pilot → production plan with a ranged estimate and a definition of done. Scope: 1-3 weeks, fixed price. Who buys: the sponsor about to fund a build (CTO/CDO, product lead). This is the antidote to estimating the unknown — it converts a risky fixed-price into a phased plan and almost always pays for itself by killing or reshaping the build.
  • AI Cost / FinOps Audit & Optimisation — Problem: "our AI bill is out of control" (GenAI bill shock) or "we don't know what our models cost." Deliverable: a TCO breakdown by driver, a cost-per-unit baseline, a prioritised optimisation list (model routing, caching, right-sizing, storage lifecycle), and a budgets-and-alerts setup. Scope: 2-4 weeks. Who buys: CTO/CFO, platform owner. Outcome is a number: "we cut your run cost X% with no quality loss" is one of the easiest ROIs to sell.
  • Build-vs-Buy Advisory for a Specific Capability — Problem: "should we build our own <capability> or buy it?" Deliverable: a decision memo applying the framework (§4) and the complexity-budget ROI from ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership, with a cost-over-time / crossover-volume model and a recommendation. Scope: 1-2 weeks. Who buys: engineering leadership facing a roadmap fork. Sell the decision, de-risked — repeatable IP you can run many times.

Buy-vs-build for the client at this stage: you are selling judgment, scoping, and de-risking — buy that from a senior practitioner rather than discovering the unknowns the expensive way. What to charge for: the discovery/audit/advisory as fixed-price packages (repeatable IP), and the staged build as phased, capped engagements. What NOT to promise: a specific accuracy/deflection number before the PoC; a fixed price for un-scoped ML; "no hallucinations / fully autonomous"; or a build cost that ignores run + maintenance. Commit to a method and a range, then narrow both as discovery and the PoC retire the unknowns.
As of 2026 — treat names/prices as perishable; re-check before quoting a client.

Tool / CategoryUse hereOSS / PaidNote (lock-in / caveat)
Cloud cost tools (AWS Cost Explorer, GCP Billing, Azure Cost Mgmt)Tag & monitor compute/storage spendPaid (native)Per-cloud; tagging discipline is the real work
FinOps platforms (Kubecost / OpenCost, Vantage, CloudZero)Cost allocation & anomaly alertsMixedNewer for GenAI; verify token-cost coverage
LLM gateways/routers (LiteLLM, OpenRouter, cloud gateways)Abstraction, routing, caching, spend capsMixedReduces lock-in and token cost; adds a hop
Eval/observability (LangSmith, Langfuse, Helicone)Bake-offs, quality + cost-per-call dashboardsMixedKeep your eval set portable
Estimation/PM (story points, T-shirt sizing in Jira/Linear)Sizing & phased trackingMixedThe discipline matters more than the tool
Pricing references (vendor pricing pages, a16z/state-of-AI)TCO & ROI inputsFreeDate everything; prices move monthly
The core "tools" of this module are a canvas, an estimate range, a phased SOW, and a cost-per-unit dashboard — process, not platform.

SituationThe heavy scoping/FinOps machinery is overkill — do this instead
A 1-2 day internal spikeSkip the SOW; time-box it and report back
Tiny, well-understood automationQuick estimate + just do it; no discovery phase needed
Spend is a few €/monthDon't build a FinOps practice; revisit at scale
One-off throwaway analysisNo production plan, no monitoring — label it disposable
Capability is an obvious commodityBuy it; the build-vs-buy memo would cost more than the decision
You genuinely know the scope coldA short fixed price beats a phased ceremony
The judgment is symmetric with the rest of the path: match the rigour of scoping and FinOps to the size of the bet. A €5k automation doesn't need a €5k discovery phase.

• McGrath & Bock — Delivering Data Analytics / agile-for-data delivery — running iterative data engagements.
  • Agrawal, Gans & Goldfarb — Prediction Machines — the economics that underpin AI ROI and qualification.
  • DeMarco & Lister — Waltzing with Bears — software risk & estimation under uncertainty; transfers directly.
  • Huyen — Designing Machine Learning Systems / AI Engineering — cost, staging, and the productionisation reality. (Paid)

• FinOps Foundation materials — the cost-discipline vocabulary (allocation, showback, unit economics).
  • Vendor pricing pages + a16z / state-of-AI reports — current cost landscape. Date everything.
  • Sculley et al., "Hidden Technical Debt in ML Systems" — why maintenance dominates TCO.

• Leadership / systems foundation this module extends → ML Systems Engineering & Technical Leadership (AI Scientist Path) — build-vs-buy basics, the complexity-budget ROI calc, stakeholder communication, and team roles.
  • System-design framing for scoping the technical solution → ML System Design (ML Engineer Path).
  • Assembling these mission patterns into a coherent service offer & operating model → Building the AI & Data Offer & Leading the Pole.

subsections.title: Decision Checklist — "Is this mission ready to scope/price?"

items.html: The outcome is a decision/action with a € number and a named owner
Data existence, access, and lawful basis are confirmed or made an explicit week-1 gate
Success has an offline metric and a business condition, agreed in writing
The work is staged PoC → pilot → production with go/no-go gates
The biggest unknown is being retired by a paid discovery before a fixed price
Cost-per-unit at expected volume is estimated (no bill-shock surprise)
A definition of done exists for every deliverable (incl. monitoring & handover)
Each AI-specific risk has a mitigation and an owner

blocks.html:
• "Just give us a fixed price" before any discovery on un-scoped ML.
  • The estimate has a precise modelling figure but hand-waves the data work.
  • The PoC's success would be a demo, with no plan or budget to productionise.
  • Token/compute cost was never modelled at production volume ("the demo was cheap").
  • Success is "use AI", or scope grows by absorption with no change requests.
  • Single-vendor dependency with no fallback and no switching-cost estimate.
  • "The model is finished" — with no monitoring, retraining, or maintenance budget.

• "If this works perfectly, what action changes, how often, and what is that worth?"
  • "What must be true about the data, and who owns getting it to us by when?"
  • "How will we both agree it worked — what number, measured how?"
  • "What's the most expensive way this can be wrong, and is a human in the loop?"
  • "Can we run a short paid discovery so I can give you a real estimate instead of a guess?"
  • "At your expected volume, what does one request/ticket cost to serve?"
  • "If the provider changes or deprecates the model, what's our plan?"
Before moving on, you should be able to:

items.html: Qualify an opportunity with a use-case canvas and a value × feasibility score, and decline AI theatre gracefully
Scope a mission as an outcome with a definition of done, staged PoC → pilot → production with real gates
Estimate under uncertainty — sizing the data work explicitly, selling discovery-first, and communicating ranges
Run a build-vs-buy and a vendor bake-off for a specific capability, lock-in entered knowingly
Build a TCO + ROI/payback case and a FinOps loop that prevents GenAI bill shock
Choose T&M / fixed / phased, staff to the phase, and run ML-adapted agile while managing expectations at three altitudes
